author: reduz

body: We don't really hash anything not aligned to words, so i think this is not needed and the code can be simplified, or this version can be kept separate for buffers if needed, but not used by default.
author: reduz

body:
The beauty of this hash function is that it is a general purpose hash function that just works on any form of data. As it is already done with several types in this PR, you can pass any type together with its size in bytes to it. For example Vector2i: `hash_murmur3_32_buffer(&p_vec, sizeof(Vector2i));`
I had dedicated versions for primitive types, but they didn't provide any performance benefit compared to this general variant. Nearly all places where dbj2 is used can be easily replaced with this buffer version. (However, at one point, I had replaced the dbj2 string hash functions (in ustring.cpp) with murmur3 but that broke ClassDB for some reason and I have no clue why).

Besides that, I am not sure if the quality of murmur3 will still be the same if I modify it to work like the dbj2 hash functions (because that is not how it was intended to be used and hash functions are quite fragile). I could do some tests, but that might take some time.
For now I added a seed parameter which defaults to the same random prime number as before.

```suggestion

				[method set_custom_format] must be called first for this [code]channel_index[/code]. formats which are not RGBA will ignore other color channels.
```

author: clayjohn

body:
I prefer to still enforce setting ``last_custom_format`` before setting the first vertex as it allows us to warn users when they are setting custom data without setting a format. 

With these changes, it is entirely possible that users will call ``set_custom`` but never call call ``set_custom_format`` in which case the custom data will be silently ignored. By forcing users to ``set_custom_format`` before adding data, we can ensure that they actually set the custom format. 

I don't see a reason why it is helpful for users to set the custom format after setting all the data anyway. In practice, you need to know your custom format before setting the data anyway, so requiring ``set_custom_format`` before the first call to ``set_vertex`` is not a big deal.

```c++
/*
 *  Summary:
 *    Virtual keycodes
 *  
 *  Discussion:
 *    These constants are the virtual keycodes defined originally in
 *    Inside Mac Volume V, pg. V-191. They identify physical keys on a
 *    keyboard. Those constants with "ANSI" in the name are labeled
 *    according to the key position on an ANSI-standard US keyboard.
 *    For example, kVK_ANSI_A indicates the virtual keycode for the key
 *    with the letter 'A' in the US keyboard layout. Other keyboard
 *    layouts may have the 'A' key label on a different physical key;
 *    in this case, pressing 'A' will generate a different virtual
 *    keycode.
```

body:
```suggestion
				Returns the value of the setting identified by [param name]. If the setting doesn't exist and [param default_value] is specified, the value of [param default_value] is returned. Otherwise, [code]null[/code] is returned.
```

You shouldn't use a line break like that in the XML docs. A line break adds a new paragraph, which is excessive for such short lines.

You should also use `[param foo]` to refer to method parameters in text, which allows it to be highlighted better, builds cognitive connection between the method signature and the description, and makes the job of translators easier when adapting the docs to another language. And it is also validated on the CI so you don't make typos, as a bonus.

For the description itself, I don't think it's correct to refer to this as "setting" a default value. You aren't setting it, you provide it as a fallback.
author: YuriSizov

body: You should look at `ProjectSettings::_get`, not at the low-level `Object` implementation. The `Object` one indeed does a lot more, but it's a general accessor for all class properties. Here we're only interested in settings, which should correctly validate with `has_setting`.

author: vitika9

body:
Source: https://bottosson.github.io/posts/colorpicker/

> Picking colors is a common operation in many applications and over the years color pickers have become fairly standardized. Ubiquitous today are color pickers based on [HSL and HSV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HSL_and_HSV). They are simple transformations of RGB values to alternative coordinates chosen to better correlate with perceptual qualities.

> Despite color picking playing a big role in a lot of applications, the design of color pickers isn’t a particularly well researched topic. While some variation exist in the widgets themselves, the choice of HSL or HSV is mostly taken for granted, with only a few exceptions.

> By far the most used color spaces today for color picking are [HSL and HSV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HSL_and_HSV), two representations introduced in the classic 1978 paper [“Color Spaces for Computer Graphics”](https://doi.org/10.1145/800248.807362). HSL and HSV designed to roughly correlate with perceptual color properties while being very simple and cheap to compute.

> Worth noting is that HSL and HSV are not quite color spaces on their own, they are transformations from a source RGB color space. For each set of RGB primaries and transfer functions, the transformation to HSL and HSV produces unique color spaces. Today HSL and HSV are most commonly used together with the sRGB color space, so that is what we will look at here and we will here use HSL and HSV to refer to HSL and HSV for the sRGB color space.

> Also useful to note is that HSL and HSV are not continuously differentiable, so that limits their use with numerical optimization and machine learning.

> One of the main advantages of HSL and HSV over the different Lab color spaces is that they map the sRGB gamut to a cylinder. This makes them easy to use since all parameters can be changed independently, without the risk of creating colors outside of the target gamut.

> We will attempt to do just that by creating new color spaces similar to HSL and HSV but that better match perception. This will be done by leveraging the Oklab color space. Using Oklab here over more advanced models such as CAM16 is useful because working out the math becomes a lot simpler. It also means that it won’t be a full color model able to adapt to different viewing conditions, but that is probably also desirable here since it is more practical.

> For consistency with the naming of Oklab, these new color spaces will be called Okhsl and Okhsv. The parameters will also be referred to as hhh, sss and lll and hhh, sss and vvv respectively. Those names are a bit confusing but I think making the new spaces easy to adopt for someone used to HSL and HSV is more important than trying to establish new names.

body:
I'm not sure if implicitly falling back to 60 FPS is a good idea. I would at least print a message in this case to warn the user that no fixed FPS was specified on the command line, and that the default value of 60 will be used instead.

Something like:

> No \`--fixed-fps \<fps\>\` argument specified on the command line. Will be recording video at 60 FPS (non-realtime).

See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/60284, which adds a project setting to set fixed FPS (and also allows toggling it at run-time). It would be interesting to make the movie writer toggleable at run-time, but it sounds difficult to do.
author: Calinou

body:
Should the number of buffer frames be adjustable with a project setting? This seems like a high value to default to – most other games I've seen default to 1024.

That said, for offline recording, we can probably perform latency compensation so it _sounds_ like audio has zero latency, even if a high number of buffer frames is used.
author: Calinou

body:
See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/1923. I think the way this PR currently handles V-Sync disabling is fine – it's just something to keep in mind for the future.

Either way, I'll definitely appreciate having this CLI argument available for games that don't expose a way to turn off V-Sync, so kudos for adding it :slightly_smiling_face: 
It's also useful to disable V-Sync on the editor without affecting the running project (at least until https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/48364 is merged).

body: A more flexible API that supports multiple codecs is desired. MJPEG is old and was beginning to be replaced by h264 in 2004. It will be used if it's the only option, but will be replaced as soon as a better option is available. If it could pipe to ffmpeg, I would immediately go with png if I needed transparency, or x264 if I wanted higher quality and lower size, especially if it was a final deliverable.

body:
In addition to that, we must always avoid blending the background color as a matte behind a transparent object.

For example, there is already a problem with MaterialEditor which uses `transparent_bg`. The following image is not shaded and should be the same color as the Color parameter, but clearly an implicit background color is blended in.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/61938263/174400622-ec016c71-c094-4393-8202-5369d51f5472.png)

When blending alphas, they should be neither a premultiplied alpha nor a matte alpha, but basically a straight alpha.

https://limnu.com/webgl-blending-youre-probably-wrong/

This can be sent as a separate Proposal from TransparentWindow as well.

body:
This PR is only going to work for a single frame, after that ``emission_tex_ofs`` isn't going to get initialized and will return garbage values. 

We need to recalculate ``emission_tex_ofs`` every frame in ``process()`` the same as we do in ``start()``. To do so, just insert the code used to generate ``emission_tex_ofs`` into the ``process()`` function.

In other words, copy:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f41cb30f9b7fe46a014162aba9c0e9a9697343d2/scene/resources/particles_material.cpp#L329-L333

and insert it here:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f41cb30f9b7fe46a014162aba9c0e9a9697343d2/scene/resources/particles_material.cpp#L490

author: Rindbee

body:
This PR indirectly limits the size:

1. Make `min_size` and `max_size` follow the size limit of the rendering server, see `Window::_clamp_limit_size()`;
2. Then make sure that `max_size_used` is not less than `min_size`, see `Window::_validate_limit_size()`.

`max_size_used` is the upper limit of actual use, it will be clamped by `min_size` and `max_size`, combined with 1, it will eventually follow the size limit of the rendering server.

`get_clamped_minimum_size()` returns the actual lower bound used, which takes into account the case when `wrap_controls` is enabled.
author: Rindbee

body:
>     2. Then make sure that `max_size_used` is not less than `min_size`, see `Window::_validate_limit_size()`.

It just ensures that when `max_size` is not as large as `min_size`, `RS::get_singleton()->get_maximum_viewport_size()` is actually used. I'm not sure whether this method returns `(0,0)`.

If the code is running in the editor, then it should use `EditorNode::get_editor_data().script_class_get_name(path)`) (something like that). EditorData maintains a reverse-lookup map to speed up these kinds of queries. Should be able to use `Engine::get_singleton()->is_editor_hint()` and the `#ifdef TOOLS_ENABLED` macro to help manipulate the contents of this method correctly.

Edit: Oh wait, actually, now that I think about it, that probably wouldn't work since this code is in core and shouldn't really be including anything from editor code. Unless other devs think it's fine so long as it has the tools enabled macro to safeguard its presence? I'm not sure.
author: willnationsdev

body:
While I believe that it would clearly make more *sense* for an `is_class` method to return the name of the best understood typename, as you have implemented, I also believe that modifying this method in this manner is too dangerous. `is_class()` is used frequently throughout the engine with the assumption that the returned class name will correspond to an actual natively-implemented class stored in the `ClassDB`. This makes it still work properly and be applicable for extensions, but not so for scripted types.

Rather than this sort of solution, I would *much* prefer to see something where we add an entirely different method to the base class that handles abstracting away the complexity of the script class name vs. the native class name. In which case, we should decide on the name of that method in the related Issue (#21789) probably. Other than that though, I like a lot of the modifications you've made, personally.

This also goes for the `is_class` method definition in the object macro further up in this same file.
author: willnationsdev

body:
I'm confused as to why adding the Script type checking to `is_class` could be a bad thing, or why a separate method would be required? This method will still work if passed a Native or Extension type.

The only possible downside/regression I can see is if a developer were to shadow an Native or Extension type with a Script type, that false-positives could happen, but shadowing is bad practice anyways.

Thank you. :)
author: Diddykonga

body: I'm unsure if adding this would make sense, It's my understanding the Editor should be separate from Core, but I believe I have seen these checks before in Core code, though I might be wrong, so I dont know lul.
author: Diddykonga

body: Yeah, I mentioned that they probably wouldn't want tool references in the core code in my edit. It's probably fine.
author: willnationsdev

body:
> I'm confused as to why adding the Script type checking to is_class could be a bad thing

I think I initially confused this with `get_class()` for some reason which is used pretty extensively in the engine source code. I don't know how often `is_class()` is used, but if it's a lot less then *maybe* it would be less problematic? Still would end up adding to the inconsistency vs. the similarly named `get_class()` though.

It's less so that it's a "bad thing" if you added this functionality, but more so that you'd need to double check every reference to this method in the rest of the codebase to ensure it's logic is still consistent should the value return `true` unexpectedly. For example, if the code passed in a typename string to verify that the given typename is the name of an inherited *native* type when (cause it needs to verify it to do some native-only operation that is incompatible with scripted types), then making this change would suddenly make it possible for previously unsupported - and correctly incompatible - types to now slip through that check. That's the "risk". We would need to ensure that things that use this method to control flow logic don't e.g. do anything with the `ClassDB` since those operations would surely fail and/or log an error after this change is made.

> why a separate method would be required?

If a separate method is defined instead, then it means no one would have to do the above "cleanup" refactoring/investigating throughout the rest of the codebase after the change is made. BUT, I do agree that it could lead to redundancy / choice paralysis or confusion regarding which method should be used in which circumstances (a reoccurring source of confusion in Godot's API for newbies imo). So, it's not that it would be required. Just that IF we made the change, we'd need a corresponding correctional sweep of the rest of the codebase to go alongside it.

>2nd part

Instead of a separate method, I would instead offer a defaulted second parameter as to whether or not Script Types are considered. It could be defaulted in C++ for internal use as false, but when exposed, defaulted to true.

The change means we are picking the format that, _in addition to_ being suited to a depth buffer, is also sampleable like a regular texture.

The latter is the piece I added. And it's needed since in D3D12 D24_S8 is an OK depth-stencil format but not sampleable, so we need the code to check for the full usage so it knows it has to pick D32_S8.

body:
If Godot supports 16k textures (16384 x 16384), this may signed-integer-overflow in the event that the raw texture is over 2GB of VRAM and the texture is RGBAH *8 bytes per pixel) or RGBAF (16 bytes per pixel) format:
```
>>> 16384*16384*8
2147483648
>>> 16384*16384*16
4294967296
```

That said, I wouldn't expect such large textures to work particularly well on any hardware due to fragmentation, but thought it would be worth a comment.

body:
One typo. Otherwise I'd also suggest moving it after the general description of what the method does. I understand the need for some warning/drama but it's dramatic enough even if it comes last ;)
```suggestion
				[b]Note:[/b] With power comes responsibility. This function should only be used by users that really know what they are doing and have a good reason for it. Forcing an immediate update of a navigation map requires locking the NavigationServer and flushing the entire NavigationServer command queue. Not only can this severely impact the performance of a game but it can also introduce bugs if used inappropriately without much foresight.
```
author: akien-mga

body:
Changing `synchronisation` to en_US spelling (twice), and typo in `save_velocity`.

I'd also suggest splitting the huge paragraph in multiple ones, if anything so that it's more digest in the docs and also in the XML. Not sure if the line breaks I suggest are the best, the idea generally is that one main concept == one paragraph.

```suggestion
				This function immediately forces synchronization of the specified navigation [code]map[/code] [RID]. By default navigation maps are only synchronized at the end of each physics frame. This function can be used to immediately (re)calculate all the navigation meshes and region connections of the navigation map. This makes it possible to query a navigation path for a changed map immediately and in the same frame (multiple times if needed).
				Due to technical restrictions the current NavigationServer command queue will be flushed. This means all already queued update commands for this physics frame will be executed, even those intended for other maps, regions and agents not part of the specified map. The expensive computation of the navigation meshes and region connections of a map will only be done for the specified map. Other maps will receive the normal synchronization at the end of the physics frame. Should the specified map receive changes after the forced update it will update again as well when the other maps receive their update.
				Avoidance processing and dispatch of the [code]safe_velocity[/code] signals is untouched by this function and continues to happen for all maps and agents at the end of the physics frame.
```

Just to illustrate my point, let me consider this case. You'll let me know if this is something that can happen in practice. | Thread A | Thread B | |---|---| | `Ref res = ResourceCache::get_ref("res://cute_tex.tres");` | | | | `Ref res = ResourceCache::get_ref("res://cute_tex.tres");` | | `if (!res.is_valid()) {` | | | | `if (!res.is_valid()) {` | | `res.instantiate();`

body:
```suggestion
		[b]Note:[/b] Using meshes to not only define walkable surfaces but also obstruct navigation baking does not always work. The navigation baking has no concept of what is a geometry "inside" when dealing with mesh source geometry and this is intentional. Depending on current baking parameters, as soon as the obstructing mesh is large enough to fit a navigation mesh area inside, the baking will generate navigation mesh areas that are inside the obstructing source geometry mesh.
```

body:
Tested this and it works overall pretty well. Great work! 
There is just one thing which does not work currently: Shortcuts (all floating windows are affected) 
Seems like they are handled by the main window, which is a regression of this PR since the floating docks which are already available (tested with beta 5) accept the shortcuts correctly when focused.

Regarding the "Make floating" button: I'm also in favor of placing it on the right, however I have no opinion on whether it should be hidden behind a ellipsis menu. Doing so would create some room for more options that can be added later on, but currently we don't need it. Maybe we could make the icon a bit more clear (although it's already pretty unambiguous) and remove the text (add it as a tooltip)? That makes it a bit more compact.

Let me explain the node layout of WindowWrapper.

```
WindowWrapper:

    window
```

body: Yeah, they would both be incorrect then. `If...true[/code],` is a dependent phrase, `the resource....bypassed` is an independent phrase, and then `the resource...anew` is a second independent phrase. Two independent phrases have to be conjoined with a conjunction preceded by a comma, so both paragraphs are missing the comma.
author: willnationsdev

body: I think I would just have something like `Map<int,RES>` for internal resources and use this always, but only load it from the cache in the main function when it first parses it.
author: reduz

body:
It's not compatible if addon plugins defining custom loaders have to include a new parameter (it's optional on call, not on override). It makes them break compat with older versions of Godot.
Besides, `no_subresource_cache` is a double-negative, there were discussions about this being bad practice during the API revamp of Godot 4.
I'm also not sure what the implementer of a custom resource is supposed to do based on this boolean: "if true, sub-resources should not be cached". Cached where? Why would they be? Is this about *sub-resources* (which can be from another file) or *built-in* resources?

body:
I had a look at the code and it looks like the PR works as advertised. Also the editor seems to work without problems from a few quick tests.
From what I can tell, the behavior doesn't change, if the newly introduced functionality (focus layers) isn't used, so it stays compatible to current behavior. However it changes the API of two functions that are exposed to GDScript.

Since #79480 tries to accomplish the same goal in a different way, I want to include a comparison of the two PRs. Both have advantages and disadvantages, so it is difficult for me to tell, which of the two approaches would be better suited.

| Topic | This PR | #79480 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Size of change | +176 −64 | +17 −10 |
| Behavior change | no, current behavior stays the same, only adds functionality | method for focusing Controls is redefined and becomes more complex |
| Chance of introducing regressions | low | medium to high |
| API change | `gui_get_focus_owner` and `gui_release_focus` change their GDScript API signature | no API change |
| Maintenance | the concept of focus layers adds complexity to Viewports and Controls | no additional concepts are introduced |
| Performance | should have no to little impact | Every group of linked Controls requires a separate SubViewport. Additional Viewports have a negative impact on rendering performance |
| Screen space real estate | Adds focus_layer to Control inspector | no changes to inspector |
| Discoverability | focus layer is introduced as new variable in the inspector, so it can be discovered easily | Can only be discovered when multiple SubViewports are used, so the functionality can be considered difficult to discover |
author: Sauermann

body: It looks like the private function `_gui_has_active_focus_layer` is used nowhere. You might as well remove it.

As discussed on rocket chat (better to keep on the PR for history purposes) : 

> About the enum / pathfinding algorithms included in the pr. I included them because I think if multiple ppl start working on different algo we may end up with x-implementations all over the place. body:
Forget what I said about mentioned delete issues. Changed the query stuff from objects to RefCounted some weeks ago so it does not suffer the same problems as the agents had e.g. from an early user `free()` or `queue_free()` while still in the waiting room.

The `result_object_id` and `callback_object_id` are also split so callbacks can happen on objects that the users needs them, not fixated to the result, e.g. while the result is updated and used by the actor the callback that the result has been updated is received on another method or the method of an entirely different object e.g. a manager object that controls the ai actors.

The parameter `callback_object_id` is also optional in the parameters compared to the agent callbacks. If no `callback_object_id` or `callback_method` the query is processed immediately as was requested in devchat.

body:
This does seem in danger of being premature optimization.

We can always move the calculation to the navigation thread later if it proves useful, but only profiling real life situations will reveal whether the user even needs to decide whether this choice needs to be made (calculating the length of the final path is unlikely to be a bottleneck, compared to say A star). Have you profiled this and found it to be a bottleneck?

Given that this is easy to retrofit, it might be better to leave this out and just calculate on demand for now. I'm all for optimization, but do recognise that simplicity for users is favoured over performance in Godot unless we have a compelling reason for a particular change.

body:
Is it intended that this makes `group` either `2^29` or `0`?

To avoid overflowing the 32-bit integer, probably better to clear the top 2 bits, e.g. `&= ~(0b11 << 29)`.

Also, does it make sense to make `child_list_id` unsigned? Non-nodes and nodes without a parent can then take `UINT32_MAX`. And it'll be easier to avoid overflowing the id since we are sure that regular `pos` is only half the domain.
author: timothyqiu

body:
I think (from memory as I wrote this a while ago) it was that it would have to load all the objects into cache again with a bunch of potential cache misses (if a large amount of objects) unnecessarily. Better to keep the operations using the same bit of memory local, as the object should already be in cache at this point.

This is theoretical, I didn't benchmark both.
author: lawnjelly

body: Well spotted this is a bug, it should be ANDing with 29 bits set. :+1:
author: lawnjelly

body:
Good thing I double checked this with calculator, it has to be `~(0b111 << 29)` or `~(0b11 << 30)`. I went for the prior just for a little safety even though probably not required. :grinning: 

It turns out `~(0b11 << 29)` is:
`1001 1111 11111111 11111111 11111111` which wouldn't have had the desired effect.
author: lawnjelly

body:
Looks good to me.

I believe the overflow when calculating `child_list_id` is destined to happen as long as the numbers are large enough. The bit operation removed quite a lot possibilities and it requires a huge number of sibling nodes to overflow now. It's relatively safe even when overflows (not undefined behavior), so I think this is okay :)
author: timothyqiu

body: Why -2?
author: RandomShaper

body:
It was probably -2 for historical reasons rather than -1 (for debugging).

The actual value (as long as it is constant) only matters in terms of the sorting relation with the nodes. If you wanted the objects to be deleted _after_ the nodes for instance you might use a high number. The negative value ensures the objects will be deleted before the nodes, and is probably one of the reasons I used `int32_t` over `uint32_t`. (If we used 0 as the constant, then there is the possibility of a node deleted before the objects, not that it probably matters that much.)

body:
This change broke Gradient's cubic interpolation mode. The first and last points use a solid color and the middle points have some kind of an off-by-one error. I tried to fix this myself but only made it worse :/

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/67974470/179372293-81218b37-7639-496a-a6ed-c9e706d053ef.png)

If it helps, I took the `cubic_interpolate()` method in `Gradient.h` that was removed in this commit and compared it to `Math::cubic_interpolate()` to pinpoint the difference. Since the arguments have different names in both functions, I replaced the first 4 with `A` `B` `C` and `D` and the fifth with `weight`. Comparing them this way made me see that the functions are not the same.

```cpp
Math::cubic_interpolate()
0.5 * ((A * 2.0) + (-C + B) * weight + (2.0 * C - 5.0 * A + 4.0 * B - D) * (weight * weight) + (-C + 3.0 * A - 3.0 * B + D) * (weight * weight * weight));

Gradient.h cubic_interpolate()
B + 0.5 * weight * (C - A + weight * (2.0 * A - 5.0 * B + 4.0 * C - D + weight * (3.0 * (B - C) + D - A)));
```

body: This is equivalent to `size` unless we failed resizing to the requested size above. Can that happen and still make a meaningful copy?
author: akien-mga

body:
Should be fine as `dest[0]` won't be evaluated [as an expression in `sizeof`](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/expressions#Unevaluated_expressions). It will only be deduced at the compile-time what the return type of `dest[0]` is. And currently it will deduce it's `int32_t` because that's what this operator returns:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f6072c9c98f11797e31a2076d17b580a6c651679/core/variant/variant_call.cpp#L755
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f6072c9c98f11797e31a2076d17b580a6c651679/core/variant/variant.h#L65
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f6072c9c98f11797e31a2076d17b580a6c651679/core/templates/vector.h#L92
author: kleonc

body:
Resizing can fail, it returns an `Error` which could be checked. Since here `dest` is being enlarged I think resizing can only fail in out of memory case. As far as I've checked `resize` will either succeed and resize to the given size or it will fail and leave the previous size unchanged. So here if `dest.resize()` will fail then it would produce an error and `dest.size()` will remain 0. Meaning if `size` was used it would try to `memcpy` to the memory being out of bounds of `dest`'s allocated memory. Using `dest.size()` prevents this, it's a good change.
I think the question is whether the error of `dest.resize()` should be checked explicitly instead (before `memcpy` is called).

Good point. I hadn't considered that resize could fail: I just wrote it that way to be defensive. I added another check:

```
ERR_FAIL_COND_V (dest.size() == 0, dest); // Avoid UB in case resize failed.
```
to handle the case that resize fails, since memcpy 0 is undefined behavior.

body:
Not directly linked to this PR but noting it down as I review the code, this doesn't need to use `TTRC` here + `TTRGET` in `_create_member_item`. Instead it could use `TTR()` here directly and just use the translated `p_type` in `_create_member_item`.

`TTRC` is used for cases where we don't want to string translated automatically, but it's just a marker for our strings extractor to get the source string - which is then translated at runtime with `TTRGET`. While `TTR` just does the translation in place, which seems suitable here (I don't see code that expects `p_type` to be the original English string).

body:
Worth noting that this seems to be the only call for `work()`, so if overriding the default value for its parameter, I question whether the parameter should have a default value in the first place (or if it should be overridden there too).

But either way we might need a comment to justify the magic number. IIUC this reduces the time allowed on each iteration to 1 ms - while the typical framerate with VSync should be 60 FPS so 16.6 ms. Isn't this significantly slowing down the search, forcing it to run over more `_process` iterations than it needs to?
The previous default of 100000 µs = 100 ms was likely too much indeed though, as it's the time for an average of 6 frames. Maybe this should be 10000 instead?

body:
> What are your results like for short searches length < 2? I'm looking at changing the behavior between short and long searches and will amend in a moment.

Indeed for searches with length < 2 it makes a big difference, 1000 faster than 100000, 10000 seems maybe fastest?

1000:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4701338/176639590-3c380462-0d3c-43be-b51e-ea1c830def50.mp4

10000:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4701338/176639607-15b38332-4866-4fb3-84f9-3d86d420882c.mp4

100000:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4701338/176639614-fe383cdd-0877-42e1-b34d-dc8430c8b2f9.mp4

But I think trying to optimize for searches with length < 2 which are practically not searches but showing the whole index is the wrong approach. The content should never need to change for such searches, so it can simply be cached the first time the EditorHelp is used, and reused whenever the search string goes below 2. Then you don't even need to add logic to handle checking if going from 0 to 1 or 1 to 0, it would likely be fast enough to just refresh the tree with precomputed data so it doesn't matter (to be confirmed).

body:
Presumably this is rare that the error flags, and I'm guessing the processing in `_try_to_find_constant_value_for_expression()` is more than the old `if (keys.has(keyName))`?

If that is the case would it be an idea to do a quick reject with the old `if (keys.has(keyName))` before calling `_try_to_find_constant_value_for_expression()`?
author: lawnjelly

body:
No, there is no need to do that. If you check the definition of  `_try_to_find_constant_value_for_expression()`, it first checks whether the current token is a constant and returns it's value. 

In fact, `_try_to_find_constant_value_for_expression()` is not a replacement of old `if (keys.has(keyName))`, it's meaning is to correctly evaluate the value of the token, so in fact it just improves that if-condition (it's still there, just few lines below).

body:
I agree with not capping the target sdk (surprisingly, gradle doesn't complain when you use insanely high values of the target sdk), but we should include a warning to let users know that the engine hasn't been tested with the specified target sdk value and may be instable.

This will let advanced users test new versions of the target sdk and report issues if they encounter any (speeding up our upgrade process), while regular users will be nudged back toward the safer (tested) versions.
author: m4gr3d

body:
> as it's typically used to support newer SDKs faster than we do with official builds

Note that customizing the target sdk build is also done when targeting other stores that don't have the play store requirements (e.g: Oculus store, Amazon Android store).
author: m4gr3d

body:
I agree, but we don't really have a good way to show such warnings to the users without being obnoxious if they actually know what they're doing.

This validation code can only add blocking errors, and while we could do a `WARN_PRINT`, it's easy to miss and will always be printed whenever they open the export dialog. Similarly, showing an info dialog in the editor would also happen every time.

We'd have to implement a custom dialog with a "Don't show this again." checkbox which saves to editor metadata for future occurrences (and somehow has logic to get reset if the conditions for the warnings change). That's significant work and IMO outside the scope of this PR.
author: akien-mga

author: TokageItLab

body: After blending algorithm was changed by #57675, `p_optimize` actually functioned only as `!sync`. `p_optimize` is replaced by `p_sync` and logical negation (`false` -> `true`) of the function arguments at each part.
author: TokageItLab

body:
Considering that `p_optimeze = !p_sync`, it seems that the default value for `p_sync` should be `false`, but in fact in most cases `p_sync = true`.

Also, there are only 2 parts that actually used that default value, `AnimationNodeBlendTree::process` and `AnimationNodeOutput::process`, which now explicitly pass `p_sync = true` instead of the default value.[^1]

[^1]: If `p_sync = false` in these parts, sync will break in the nested cases such as `BlendTree` within `BlendTree` and `BlendTree` within `BlendSpace` etc.
author: TokageItLab

body:
This section should only be processed at the moment of playback or seeking. With this change, this is always processed even though it is in DC mode, so this implementation is wrong. In my opinion, we probably need to implement a special case for DC mode when firing `play()`.

Also, it seems to me that this should not be processed here as long as it is in DC mode, even at seek time in the first place. It should use `track_find_key()` or `value_track_get_key_indices()` instead of `value_track_interpolate()`, as in the else block.
author: TokageItLab
pfertyk

body: You must use `else if` because the track will never have more than one `update_mode` at the same time. Multiple `if`s cause unnecessary evaluations.
author: TokageItLab
pfertyk

body:
@TokageItLab  It took me a moment to realize that you probably mean just the expression I've added :) I've changed it to `else if` to the previous handling of CAPTURE mode, and left the if/else if sequence below untouched. Is that OK?

My question, however, was about a different thing. I've noticed that there is usually an `else` clause for the `if (pa->accum_pass != accum_pass)` expression in other places here, but I couldn't figure out what I should put there for the DISCRETE mode. All the examples used value interpolation in the `else if`, but that's not the case for DISCRETE (in this mode, we don't interpolate). So, I've moved the assignment of the value outside of `if` whatsoever, and therefore there is no `else`. Is that OK?
author: pfertyk

Note that this only allows unregistering protocols specific to this user account.

I'm not sure what would happen if we tried to disallow this, such as what would happen if you tried to register over an existing protocol, and what would happen if the app ID changes? body:
Tested it locally and it seems fine. I did not see cases where implicit conversion would happen unwittingly.

Some tests:
```gdscript
var a1 = "42"
assert(a1 is String)
#var a2 : int = "42"  # Value of type "String" cannot be assigned to a variable of type "int"
var a3 : int = "42" as int
assert(a3 is int)
var a4 := "42"
assert(a4 is String)
var a5 := "42" as int
assert(a5 is int)
#a5 + a4  # Invalid operands "int" and "String" for "+" operator
var a6 = int("42")
assert(a6 is int)
```
```gdscript
var a1 = "42.5"
assert(a1 is String)
#var a2 : float = "42.5"  # Value of type "String" cannot be assigned to a variable of type "float"
var a3 : float = "42.5" as float
assert(a3 is float)
var a4 := "42.5"
assert(a4 is String)
var a5 := "42.5" as float
assert(a5 is float)
#a5 + a4  # Invalid operands "float" and "String" for "+" operator
var a6 = float("42.5")
assert(a6 is float)
```

body:
Actually, I ran this in debug mode and I do believe that it makes sense! :)

The function changed is already specifically for attribute lookups. The changes make sure that if what comes after the `.` is basically text (the call to `is_node_name()`), then it should be interpreted as an identifier. Again, _only_ specifically within the context of an attribute lookup, i.e. `base.attribute`.

Anything else that isn't text, when `is_node_name()` returns false, will still raise an error due to the `consume` function wanting to see an identifier there.

body:
```suggestion
		int ysort_p_abs_z_index; // Absolute Z index of parent. Only populated and used when Y-sorting.
```

Does `p` here stand for "parent"? Might be worth spelling out in full as we usually use `p_` prefix for parameters, and in some of the other code you have e.g. `orig_p_z = p_z` and it's not clear whether you mean it as "original value of the `p_z` parameter" or "original value of parent Z index". If the former, we usually don't repeat the `p_` in local variables, it's meant specifically to indicate that the identifier is that of a method parameter (so it would be `orig_z = p_z` for example).

body:
> I'll try and get this fixed today

Well that was a long day 🙃, thanks for your patience

So the problem was from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/64271. Adding an error message to the log requires access to its theme items (e.g. error icon). https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/060d0ce88204f9180beb3764048975e9dcf5c496/modules/gltf/register_types.cpp#L99 is getting called before the EditorLog has entered the SceneTree. This means `NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGED` won't have been called yet, and the EditorLog's theme cache won't have been initialized, hence the crash. Fortunately, the log is already rebuilt anyway  on `NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGE`, so I added this check to simply `return` early if the EditorLog hasn't entered the scene yet. Theoretically this should be more performant anyway since it won't have to add the log line twice.

It's the same thing as https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/e06cd2742f8af57b23fa11dec62ba9c4eb544291/editor/plugins/texture_editor_plugin.cpp#L41-L49

`NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGED` is the only `case` here, and it should now only be called when the node is inside of the tree, so this check is redundant.

I tried to document it pretty well here:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/74eb2a70bd7ab881776b30fb34bf98d03651bf52/doc/classes/Control.xml#L1177-L1184

Previously `NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGED` was also getting called from `remove_child_notify` which I changed so it won't anymore. That's what caused the crash from #32985 and #50702. It actually didn't used to be this way a long time ago:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/259418f8275371fc2f0bab6892caa4ef66b84240/scene/gui/control.cpp#L425-L450
Note how `notification(NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGED)` is commented out. At some point `add_child_notify` and `remove_child_notify` were changed to use `_propagate_theme_changed`, which always send `NOTIFICATION_THEME_CHANGED` regardless of where it's called from.

body:
Part of the issue here is that that global shader uniforms hashmap takes any RID. I.e. it doesn't check if the RID is for a geometry instance. Here you are passing an RID for the mesh that the Geometry Instance uses. Accordingly, if to MeshInstances use the same mesh, the check fails for the second MeshInstance and the instance uniform does not get updated below. 

I think you will need to store a ``->self`` value in ``RenderGeometryInstanceBase`` so you can access the RID of the GeometryInstance itself
author: clayjohn

body:
```suggestion
			Scales the 3D render buffer based on the viewport size uses an image filter specified in [member rendering/scaling_3d/mode] to scale the output image to the full viewport size. Values lower than [code]1.0[/code] can be used to speed up 3D rendering at the cost of quality (undersampling). Values greater than [code]1.0[/code] are only valid for bilinear mode and can be used to improve 3D rendering quality at a high performance cost (supersampling). See also [member rendering/anti_aliasing/quality/msaa_3d] for multi-sample antialiasing, which is significantly cheaper but only smooths the edges of polygons.
```
author: clayjohn

body:
```suggestion
			Scales the 3D render buffer based on the viewport size uses an image filter specified in [member ProjectSettings.rendering/scaling_3d/mode] to scale the output image to the full viewport size. Values lower than [code]1.0[/code] can be used to speed up 3D rendering at the cost of quality (undersampling). Values greater than [code]1.0[/code] are only valid for bilinear mode and can be used to improve 3D rendering quality at a high performance cost (supersampling). See also [member ProjectSettings.rendering/anti_aliasing/quality/msaa_3d] for multi-sample antialiasing, which is significantly cheaper but only smooths the edges of polygons.
```
author: clayjohn

body: WebGL2 uses Renderbuffers to support Multisampled Framebuffers. So you can still use it even though ``GL_TEXTURE_2D_MULTISAMPLE`` isn't defined
author: clayjohn

body: We need to minimize the number of FBO changes and full screen copies we do in order to keep performance manageable on mobile devices. Accordingly, I would put the resolve inside of ``render_target_copy_to_back_buffer()``. If copying the full rect, you can get away with resolving the Multisampled buffer directly into the backbuffer. If copying a partial rect, you'll have to resolve and then copy still.
author: clayjohn

body: We should put together a list of what objects users can expect to see AA on and which they won't. For example, users shouldn't expect AA either within or on the edges of sprites. But they may see AA on the edges of sprites where the Texture us solid and goes right to the edge of the quad.
author: clayjohn

body:
Shouldn't MSAA work on the edges of sprites with opaque borders (at least if they're rotated)? Sprites are rects after all, and they're probably drawn with 2 triangles behind the scenes.

To avoid confusion, what I'd mention instead is that MSAA will not smooth out nearest-neighbor filtered textures, as well as shader-induced aliasing. If you need smooth nearest-neighbor filtering, use a shader like https://godotshaders.com/shader/sub-pixel-accurate-pixel-sprite-filtering/ (2D) or https://godotshaders.com/shader/smooth-3d-pixel-filtering/ (3D).
author: Calinou

body:
Currently combined 2D and 3D rendering is broken since the resolve attachment's store op is `VK_ATTACHMENT_STORE_OP_DONT_CARE` while it should be `VK_ATTACHMENT_STORE_OP_STORE`. (this took way too long to figure out :)) 
![grafik](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50084500/181396214-0f0a0ad1-e133-4d59-ab2c-bb657def724e.png)
Just brute forcing all store ops to `VK_ATTACHMENT_STORE_OP_STORE` resolves the issue:
![grafik](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50084500/181395672-4c1893d6-2284-46ef-b13c-7e8eec448bed.png)
Now that I know what's wrong I can work on a proper fix, will probably push one tomorrow after I addressed clayjohns review comments. (I also rewrote a few bits to make things cleaner)

Other platforms do not send `unicode == 0x09` for the Tab presses, so it might work on Windows but only on Windows. It's also might cause issues with unexpected Unicode values from `Backspace`, and it will make Windows behavior inconsistent with other platforms.

There's already `keycode` to handle Tab, ~so it's probably better to do something like this instead:~ Actually, it's probably better to remove `|| k->get_keycode() == Key::TAB` part to fix #27036 for the `TextEdit`.

But I'm not sure if it should be changed in the base `TextEdit` at all, there's `CodeEdit` subclass that's properly handling indentation (including Shift+Tab and indenting multiple lines, which will not work with this approach).

I feel like we could do this a lot more simply just by exposing a way to set ``rect_dirty`` to ``true`` every time that a bone is updated instead. 

Although I guess the only way to do that is for skeleton's to store a reference to the CanvasItems that use them the way Skeleton's do in 3D. Then we could easily have a callback in``update_dirty_skeletons()`` like we do in 3D https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/c55e8dd51616b658850fb899897e8ef7a5d3fc3a/drivers/gles3/rasterizer_storage_gles3.cpp#L5328-L5330

author: lawnjelly

body:
For future reference, Lawnjelly and I discussed this at length over RocketChat. I was leaning towards exposing a callback like what is done in 3D. I felt this would minimize the runtime cost and avoid the complexity inside ``get_rect`` however. Once I started implementing the callbacks. It turned out that there would be significant added complexity as skeleton's would start to have to manage their Items, and due to some dependency issues between RasterizerStorage and RasterizerCanvas.

As we couldn't decide which approach would be the costlier approach at run time. We have decided to stick with this approach as it requires no architectural changes and will be easier to debug should issues arise.

body:
The `valid` variable is only updated to `true` when the templates are valid. Since for the Android port, we're skipping the templates' checks, it's never updated to `true`, and thus always returns `false`.

For non-Android port, the initial value for `valid` is always overwritten, so there's no risk of affecting the logic for the other platforms.
author: m4gr3d

body:
Yeah but it seems to be a workaround here. This method checks if export templates are valid. If they're not valid on PC, it should behave the same as if they're not valid on Android. With this change it will be `true` on Android and `false` on PC, and the latter seems more accurate to me.

Sounds like what you want to do is just enable PCK export, and the problem here is that the logic to enable it is coupled to the availability of templates. Which is partially true at least for Mono (BCL needs to be included in the PCK), but can also not be a deal-breaker for simple projects.

So what's needed IMO is a bigger refactor that decouples PCK export check from binary export check. Or if a temporary hack is needed for Android, it can simply be not to call `can_export` since your changes basically makes it a no-op.
author: akien-mga

body:
> Or is that in a scope that the file manager apps aren't allowed to access

Correct, the user data folder is in an app-private directory provided by [`Context.getFilesDir()`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/content/Context#getFilesDir()) that's not accessible to file manager.

The alternative would be to move that directory to the one provided by [`Context.getExternalFilesDir(...)`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/content/Context#getExternalFilesDir(java.lang.String)).
The tradeoff being that directory may not be available on low-end devices, or may be slower (e.g: removable sdcard).
author: m4gr3d

body: Fixed.
author: m4gr3d

body:
> This method checks if export templates are valid. If they're not valid on PC, it should behave the same as if they're not valid on Android. With this change it will be true on Android and false on PC, and the latter seems more accurate to me.

The method unfortunately is a catch-all for export templates validity checks, and project configuration validity checks.
For a full binary export, both checks are valid. However for a `pck/zip` only export, the export templates validity checks are irrelevant.
So given we'll only (initially) support `pck/zip` export for the Android editor, the export templates validity checks are being disabled. So yes in this scenario, if the project configuration validity checks fail, they fail both on Android and Windows. If the export templates validity checks fail, they only fail on Windows but not on Android.

> Which is partially true at least for Mono (BCL needs to be included in the PCK), but can also not be a deal-breaker for simple projects.

The flow I'm imagining is that a user would do development and testing using the Android editor port, then via export of a zip or pck file, remote versioning or file syncing, continue and finish the release process on a fully featured platform. Would the BCL need to be included for that workflow to work?

 >  Or if a temporary hack is needed for Android, it can simply be not to call can_export since your changes basically makes it a no-op.

The `can_export` call is still needed for the project configuration validity checks. This particular method is overwritten by the `EditorExportPlatformWindows::can_export` method which uses this parent logic for its template validation logic, so the short-circuit for Android is still needed.

> So what's needed IMO is a bigger refactor that decouples PCK export check from binary export check.

Agree this would be a good approach as well. Splitting `can_export` into a `validate_export_templates` and a `validate_project_configuration` methods. We can then skip the call to the `validate_export_templates` for the Android editor.

body: This API suggests polling usage.  I would make this take an input array of task ids and an output array of statuses.  That way, a serious caller waiting for many tasks can query them all and only get hit for mutex thread context switch once.  The simple case of a single task could be covered by that.

body:
This is not what I expected.  I thought the worker thread would be external to this.  

Specifically, let's say I allocate 5 of these objects, because I am using software from various sources and they each have one.   On a console platform, Godot would need to service them all using just a few threads.  On a big PC, you might want to use dedicated threads.
author: derammo

body:
The optimization comment is regarding using WaitForMultipleObjects (Windows) or similar primitives that allow you to blocking wait on both task_available_semaphore and done_semaphore at the same time.  That allows the thread to not busy wait and give up the CPU via microsleeping, and instead actually get descheduled exactly until the wait completes.

A Linux equivalent (ancient) would be poll against multiple eventfd.  Not sure what the modern equivalent of that is.
author: derammo

body:
This will need careful user documentation.  There may be user task code on the stack, as tasks can wait on other tasks.  User task code must be fully re-entrant for this to not lead to self-deadlock on the user side. 

For example, Task X's task function includes submitting Task Y and then waiting on it.   Without the body of Task X going off the stack, this wait can execute Task Z on the same thread.  That may be the same function as Task X or another one.   Either way, the user must understand they can't hold locks or have any "incomplete" shared state when they call "wait_for_task_completion."   It is a great simplification to avoid deadlock on the worker thread side, but it will need careful usage from the user.
author: derammo

body: This isn't really an error.   It should be possible to submit a bunch of long running tasks and then exit the program.  For that to work, you'd need an additional (optional) Callable in add_task(...) so that there is a separate function to call for cancellation.   At shut down, all pending tasks get called on the cancellation callback.  Waiting on a task that was canceled must then also survive without error, because some of the user code may at the same time call wait_for_task_completion(...) during the shutdown process, based on timing.   If ThreadRunner was RefCounted, you could probably just keep all the task objects that were canceled, with the semaphores posted.   Then these deallocate only when all customers of the ThreadRunner are gone.
author: derammo

body: If you check this inside _process_task() and then early return with a bool return value to indicate the thread should exit instead, then this doesn't even need to be atomic.  In the normal case, you check this atomic, and it is false.  Then you call _process_task() which 100% of the time grabs the mutex.   In all cases except on exit, the atomic access isn't useful and is just another synchronization point.
author: derammo

body: Concretely, maybe this object is an internal object only, and the user-visible object is a shim that is assigned to one of N such internal pools.   On a thread restricted platform, all shims would map to the same pool (N=1, with the number of threads set to the platform limit.)  On a large platform, you might allocate several internal pools if you want to always allocate reserved threads to different functional areas.   Not sure you need that, as the OS will likely do a decent job keeping things fair if you just put a lot of threads in the pool on a large platform.
author: derammo

body: Game folks might like the idea though of saying "3 threads for this type of task, 7 threads for this type of task."   That would only do anything on a large platform, where the user object actually has its own thread pool.

author: derammo

body:
More context in case it is useful:

An async API should always have a guarantee of _exactly_ one callback.   This is so that user code can allocate stuff for the async request, stick it in user data, and clean it up on callback.   When the system shuts down, this state needs to be cleaned up, but it may not be possible to execute the queued work, because the system is going down.  

Additionally, it is just slow to have shut down wait for a bunch of work that nobody is going to use anyway.  That's why there normally is a callback parameter that tells you if you are supposed to just clean up or actually do your work, or you can have an additional callback (Callable) to call if the work item / task gets canceled before running.  Because you can't force GD clients to have a particular callback signature, I think the optional Callable is the better way here.  In that case, the guarantee will be that either your work function gets called or the cancellation callback.  Either way, you know you can clean up whatever related stuff you may have.

author: fabriceci

body:
> I don't know why it's tested this way either, I wonder if the test is not weak, for example if there's diagonal 2 0 1, this test might detect it as an identity matrix `(Math::abs(rows[0][0] + rows[1][1] + rows[2][2] - 3) < angle_epsilon`

@fabriceci Not gonna lie, I haven't noticed this last condition before. :laughing: And yeah, it looks absolutely incorrect.

> I just wonder if the first condition (just before the identity one) can be replaced by `is_diagonal` and then we can skip the diagonal test and only check for the 1.

@fabriceci The first condition is meant to check whether the matrix is [singular](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/SingularMatrix.html), not whether it's [diagonal](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/DiagonalMatrix.html). So no, the first check can't be replaced by `is_diagonal` call.

However, the current check for singularity seems strange/incomplete? :thinking: I mean matrix can be singular / not invertable if this condition isn't met, trivial example:
```
0 0 0
1 2 3
4 5 6
```
So I guess the question is whether there are some assumptions about the actual matrix of the `Basis`. Maybe such matrix is assumed to be invalid / it's assumed to be impossible to obtain such data in `Basis`. But if there are no restrictions then this check would need to be broader. In theory a check for determinant being zero should be sufficient.

We have multiple options to optimize space usage of animation.

1. Uncompressed
1. animation track optimizer corrupts the animation
1. bitpacking compression cannot be modified
1. reducing the frame rate corrupts the animation

Choosing uncompressed animations means out of the box animation keyframe takes storage.

Perhaps we can develop other options like force bitpack on export similar to tscn export scn.

I do prefer correct animations that doesn't require quality checks. So either uncompressed or bitpacked at export can work.

@reduz any advice on this?

body:
I think the new approach is too close to something we already have with `EditorData::add_move_array_element_function` and `EditorInspectorArray::setup_with_move_element_function`, covering the exact same use case but from the object directly instead of an editor plugin.

I think this PR is incomplete and needs few changes:
- Remove EditorData::add_move_array_element_function, and replace it with the new approach where it is used.
- The moving/adding/removing function (here add and swap) should be arguments of `EditorInspectorArray::setup_with_move_element_function`, as that is what this function was built for.

On a side note, I don't like much having to provide a swap function. Having only a swapping function will mean that it will have to be called a lot when you would need to move an element from one place to another. Depending on the underlying structure used to store the array, this can have a significant performance impact, especially when you are using Vector and/or need to update some side data structure (like a cache for example, or something like that). This is particularly the case for TileSet data layers for example.
Instead, the function should required to be called only once for each operation you need to do, so that those sort of underlying updates can be only triggered once per operation.

So what I suggest instead is to replace the `swap` function by a `move` function, so instead of swapping two elements, it takes one element and move it to another position. We can keep the same logic for `p_element_index < 0` and `p_to_pos < 0` to respectively to add and remove elements. This should avoid any performance issue.

body:
It is indeed somewhat arbitrary but this should be the default install path for a distro-packaged mold.

We can't use `which mold` as this gives `/usr/bin/mold`, but for the `-B` linker option GCC expects a path to a folder with a file named `ld` (yes that's software designed in the 90s...):
```
$ ls -l /usr/libexec/mold/
total 0
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 14 Jul  1 10:45 ld -> ../../bin/mold*
```

Expand "A classic way to use mold" in https://github.com/rui314/mold#how-to-use for details.

Possible install locations for this would be:
- `/usr/libexec/mold` if using `PREFIX=/usr`, which is what most Linux distros would do when packaging
- `/usr/local/libexec/mold` if using `PREFIX=/usr/local`, which is the default and hence what users who self-install mold from source might have
- Any arbitrary location if users pass a different `PREFIX` or hack the `Makefile`

I chose to only support the distro package scenario as the rest is IMO too difficult to handle. We could maybe at least do a check that `/usr/libexec/mold/ld` or `/usr/local/libexec/mold/ld` exist and use it, or abort.

body:
This PR is totally wrong. The calculation itself is wrong and the path and interpolation is completely crazy since it doesn't care flip problem. You can see it in the sample project in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/57951.

The exp/log calculation is already implemented correctly and does not need to be changed, I say again that the only remaining problem is the sign flip issue https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/57951#issuecomment-1046425597. Even if we calculate instantaneous shorter paths, the sign changes when the interval changes, which breaks the path calculation, so we need to cache the sign of the first calculation in some ways.

body:
I removed the checks so now `lto=thin` can be used with any linker, since they all seem to support it.

We'd need to benchmark both build times and performance to see which one should be recommended for each platform/compiler/linker combination.
author: akien-mga

body: "for production builds" sounds a bit dogmatic. Perhaps it would be better to write "slow, for production builds" to at least explain why it's only used in these cases? I know that it also makes debugging useless, but I can see LTO being used for development purposes too like benchmarking and whatnot.
author: deralmas

body: I see that in your other build system PR you specified the selected jobs for LTO. Couldn't you do the same in the other platforms too?
author: deralmas

body: Why force GCC stuff here? I see that this was moved, but now GCC stuff will be always forced on instead of using the system utilities, even without LTO.
author: deralmas

body: Is this wanted? It looks like a debug statement to me.
author: deralmas

body: That's just a hint for when to use it, it doesn't mean you can't use it for other use cases. I think it's fine as is personally.
author: akien-mga

body: Clang doesn't support it.
author: akien-mga

body: I just moved it, it seemed weird to force this for LTO and not for non-LTO. I can move it back but this hacky either way, requires more work.
author: akien-mga

body: Indeed, thanks. I'll remove it.
author: akien-mga

body:
No worries, this stuff's subjective (and also a huge nitpicking). Still, thinking about it the style's consistent with the rest of the messages, so I'll mark this as resolved since it actually makes sense to keep it as is IMO.

Edit: Forgot I couldn't lmao, consider it resolved from my side.
author: deralmas

body:
I see why you did it, but I think that the idea was to force GCC's `ranlib` and `ar` just when LTOing since that's required by GCC for it.

From the gcc man page:
```
           If object files containing GIMPLE bytecode are stored in a library
           archive, say libfoo.a, it is possible to extract and use them in an
           LTO link if you are using a linker with plugin support.  To create
           static libraries suitable for LTO, use gcc-ar and gcc-ranlib
           instead of ar and ranlib; to show the symbols of object files with
           GIMPLE bytecode, use gcc-nm.  Those commands require that ar,
           ranlib and nm have been compiled with plugin support.  At link
           time, use the flag -fuse-linker-plugin to ensure that the library
           participates in the LTO optimization process:

                   gcc -o myprog -O2 -flto -fuse-linker-plugin a.o b.o -lfoo
```
author: deralmas

body: Thanks for the details, I'll move it back :+1:

body:
Had to move the block after parsing the platform `configure` so that `methods.using_clang(env) or methods.using_emcc(env)` works (on Linux/Windows it depends on handling `use_llvm=yes` in `configure`).

As a side note, we really need better code to handle knowing which toolchain we're using.

body: Testing it on Mac right now, full LTO is 5 min into linking and it already using 19 GB of ram and hitting the swap, so I guess it will take a while.
author: bruvzg

body: Yell full LTO seems really slow with LLVM as it doesn't use parallelism. ThinLTO seems to be a much better option for LLVM so current version of that PR defaults to it with `production=yes`.
author: akien-mga

body:
Done some testing with editor `release-debug` builds on ARM64 macOS, and do a comparison for the performance of opening large project in the editor, and stripped executable sizes:

| LTO       | Link time (real) | Link time (CPU time) | Memory used by linker | Executable size | Speed improvement |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|-----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| No LTO    | < 1 min          | < 1 min              | ~ 500 MB              | 109 MB          | Reference         |
| Full LTO  | 2:30 h           | 1:20 h               | ~ 48 GB               | 120 MB          | ~ 1-2%            |
| Thin LTO  | 16 min           | 1:50 h               | ~ 6 GB                | 132 MB          | ~ 1%              |

Seems like LTO is almost entirely useless.
author: bruvzg

body:
Woah. That's pretty surprising numbers. But definitely seems like something we should keep off for macOS.

If you still have the builds around, it could be worth checking the impact on performance for GDScript, as on Linux/Windows last we compared we got some pretty good numbers for LTO.

I guess we can set `("lto", "none")` default option for macOS like done for iOS, but keep it exposed for those who want to experiment?
author: akien-mga

body: I think adding the option to experiment makes sense, but make sure to print a warning if it's enabled on macOS.
author: Calinou

body: Tested it with GDScript, results are similar, about 2% improvement. I'll re-test it with the x86_64 versions later, maybe it's M1 only case.
author: bruvzg

body: In this method, neither `epsilon` nor `tolerance` are correct names. The `Execute` method parameter name is `delta`, and we are multiplying it by 10, so this is really just "a tenth of the delta given to the Clipper library".
author: aaronfranke

body: This is also suspicious. In `reduce` the parameter name is currently called `epsilon`, and then this does some weird math and passes it as an `optimization` parameter. It doesn't look like a comparison epsilon/tolerance.
author: aaronfranke

body: A hint that this could be wrong is that epsilon values should be approx 0.000001, but this defaults to 2.0.

body:
What's the difference between `rename_gdscript_functions` and `check_for_rename_gdscript_functions` btw? Their code seems almost identical (except for the return value).

EDIT: I can see there's a check in `project_converter_3_to_4.cpp:1665` which differentiates `.gd` and `.tscn` files. Should we probably introduce an extra argument to `rename_gdscript_functions` such as `is_builtin`? This way we could control the behavior of quote escaping inside `rename_gdscript_functions`. It would also make sense to call `rename_gdscript_functions` inside the `.tres` branch several lines below (since resources may also contain built-in scripts).
author: and3rson

body:
`check_for_rename_gdscript_functions` additionally prints exactly in which line change will happen.
At beginning it was very different than `rename_gdscript_functions`, but I unified this one day, so there is a lot of duplicated code, which could be extracted to another function, but when I tried to do it I failed due some strange Regex memory leaks(this shouldn't be too hard to fix)

I think that adding `is_builtin` is good solution, but this probably will be only needed by custom renames that use " inside regex e.g.
```
OS.window_fullscreen = true -> ProjectSettings.set("display/window/size/fullscreen",true)
```
but not with
```
sort_custom( a , b ) -> sort_custom(Callable( a , b ))
```

body:
```C++
inspector_only = !inspector_only
```
If `inspector_only` is true, this will always disable it.

```C++
editor_plugin_screen != nullptr && editor_plugin_screen->get_name() == "Script"
```
Instead of this, you could do `ScriptEditor::get_singleton()->is_visible_in_tree()` ([that's how it was done previously](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/838cb598e3ef5572018d3d549c15bb27b0a5864d/editor/scene_tree_dock.cpp#L1384)).
author: KoBeWi

body:
`inspector_only = !inspector_only`

My intention here is to not override `inspector_only` when it's already `true`.

Say `node_select_toggles_inspector_only == false` and `inspector_only == true`, then `inspector_only` will be overriden to `false` (this is based on the assumption that there are already situations where `inspector_only` may be true, so we avoid setting it to `false` in those situations).

With my intention, we try to override it to `true` only when a context switching was already going to take place (`inspector_only == false`), thus avoiding possible regressions.

---

`ScriptEditor::get_singleton()->is_visible_in_tree()`

Cool, thanks.

body: What if this was calculated in `_update_dimensions` instead? I think that'd make more sense logically, and then we wouldn't need a `mutable` here
author: AaronRecord

body:
It needs to traverse all the child controls to calculate it.

The main purpose of `ScrollContainer::update_scrollbars()` is to confirm the status (`max`/`page`/`visible`) of scrollbars, it needs the largest one of the minimum size of child controls (`child_max_size`) already have been determined.

`ScrollContainer::_update_dimensions()` mainly determines the `rect` of the child controls. The `rect` needs to be calculated according to the `value` of the `ScrollBar`s.

So in terms of dependencies, it is unlikely to calculate all the data in one loop. Two loops are required. 

For convenience, it is better to calculate in `get_minimum_size()`. As the containers call `update_minimum_size()` before calling `queue_sort()`. See `*_child_notify(Node *p_child)` in `Container`.
author: Rindbee

body:
A while since I did any enum binding, but do we need to bind a `MAX`?
Is it useful to users, and does it have any implications for when new values are added to the enum?

(Speaking as a user this was kind of confusing, as if metric MAX might give the max FPS rather than the max of the enum.)
author: lawnjelly

body:
> A while since I did any enum binding, but do we need to bind a `MAX`?

It's not a strict requirement, but we generally do it for exposed enums. It allows users to loop over the size of the enum or implement cycling logic without hardcoding the size of the enum, as there is no reflection in GDScript on C++ enums. Reflection is supported on GDScript enums though, since they're internally dictionaries.

```gdscript
for metric in range(Engine.FPS_METRIC_MAX):
	pass

# Or:
var fps_metric: Engine.FPSMetric = Engine.FPS_METRIC_AVERAGE

func _input(event: InputEvent) -> void:
	if event.is_action_pressed("ui_accept"):
	fps_metric = wrapi(fps_metric + 1, 0, Engine.FPS_METRIC_MAX)
```

> and does it have any implications for when new values are added to the enum?

No, we can safely change its value without breaking compatibility. It's one exception to enum value changes since we assume people are not relying on the `_MAX` value for anything else than looping/iteration.

> (Speaking as a user this was kind of confusing, as if metric MAX might give the max FPS rather than the max of the enum.)

When the time comes to add a maximum metric, it'll be `FPS_METRIC_MAXIMUM` to avoid a conflict, and will be listed before `FPS_METRIC_MAX` (since that one always remains at the end of the enum).

body:
Each SNAME uses unique memory, even if they contain the same string.

By "2 instances of this button" I meant that this particular line is executed only twice per theme change. SNAME is supposed to avoid StringName creation, because it's slow, but it only really matters for cases where it's called very often (e.g. all inspector controls should use SNAME, because they are instantiated with every inspector refresh, which affects responsiveness a lot). ControlEditorPopupButton is created only once per editor lifetime and only updates on theme change, which is rare, so it doesn't need SNAME.

body:
The problem is that its not on/off. There are two additional things to consider here:
1) when it is set to `XRMODE_DEFAULT` we need a value that is different in many places, when we're checking if we need to compile XR shaders, we check the shader setting, if we check whether OpenXR needs to be setup, its checking that value, we'll soon be adding whether OpenXR needs to be setup in the editor. Finally when we want to run XR in the editor the default mode checks an editor setting. So each part of the system that checks the XR mode will react differently if the mode is set to default.
2) once I start on the editor I want to switch between two ways of setting up the editor, a mode where you're purely running the editor in VR (which is the only mode available on stand alone headsets) and a mode where you still have the normal desktop UI combined with the in headset UI.

That said, at the moment I am thinking of removing the XRMODE_MIXED and just switch the scenario based on whether you're running stand alone VR or PCVR.

body:
@akien-mga but that doesn't work because when XRMODE_DEFAULT is set, they check different switches.

OpenXRAPI checks whether OpenXR needs to be enabled (i.e. `GLOBAL_GET("xr/openxr/enabled")`, hate the need to have this one but seeing OpenXR needs to take ownership of Vulkan we are living with this PITA setting)
RenderingCompositor checks whether the XR shader setting is enabled (i.e. `GLOBAL_GET("xr/shaders/enabled")`)

And in the near future the editor will check:
- an editor specific setting that tells us if the editor should run in XR mode (likely `EDITOR_GET("xr/enabled")`)
- whether OpenXR needs to be enabled in the editor (likely `GLOBAL_GET("xr/openxr/editor_enabled")`, though I may change enabled into a mode).

Setting XRMODE_ON overrides all these settings and enables them all. XRMODE_OFF turns them all off.

Hence the need for this check to be implemented **differently** in all this situations.
author: BastiaanOlij

body:
The XRMODE_OFF use case is just to shut everything off, so people can create an app that they setup with the proper settings to use it for XR, but then can shut everything off to also start it in desktop mode.
The XRMODE_ON use case is mostly the editor, as it always turns on OpenXR you cant really use it if you're using a different SDK like ARKit or ARCore.

body: We don't use `const` for primitive type function arguments either, so please remove it. (We do use `const` for Object-derived function arguments when possible still.)
author: Calinou

body:
> We don't use `const` for primitive type function arguments either, so please remove it. (We do use `const` for Object-derived function arguments when possible still.)

Roger that, but why is this bad? I tell the compiler additional information, also additional information for the developer at any rate.
author: Jerrody

body:
It's fine to use `const` for primitive type parameters in your own projects, but we considered it redundant since we already have a `p_` prefix for function parameters. (This isn't enforced by clang-format as there's no option to do that.)

By the way, it looks like the expected `p_` prefix for function parameters is missing here.

```suggestion
VkResult VulkanContext::create_render_pass2(const VkDevice p_device, const VkRenderPassCreateInfo2 *p_create_info, const VkAllocationCallbacks *p_allocator, VkRenderPass *p_render_pass) {
```

It's better to rename parameters to match the usual Godot code style, and it will prevent shadowing.
author: bruvzg

body: You have removed `const` from `p_color` in the declaration, but not from the implementation. Most of the codebase use const reference for color (`const Color &p_color`). So it should be better to use it here as well, for consistency, but it's questionable if there’s any benefit from it (`Color` if `float c[4]`, so depending on platform ABI, it's probably passed in a single XMM register or similar).
author: bruvzg

body:
> Roger that, but why is this bad?

It's not bad, but in this case it's mostly a style thing. `const` is important for pointers, references, and non-POD structs. But for primitive type arguments, the compiler is restricted by ABI and there is pretty much nothing to optimize. And it's not critical information for the developers, whether it's changed or not does not affect anything.

author: Calinou

body:
```suggestion
			If [code]true[/code], the current audio driver will be automatically disabled when the application is paused by the OS, and re-enabled when the application is resumed.
			[b]Note:[/b] This only affects platforms where the OS can pause applications (typically Android and iOS). This does not refer to in-game pausing, where you can manually mute audio by calling [method AudioServer.set_enabled].
```
author: Calinou

body:
Tested locally on Linux and macOS, it works as expected. Code looks good to me.

On Linux, I can't really spot a noticeable difference in CPU utilization on my 9950X3D with a debug build of 3.x. It's pretty low already (it oscillates between 0% and 1.9%, likely because of filesystem scanning).

On macOS 15.5 on a M4 Max, CPU utilization when the editor is on an empty Script tab (and unfocused) drops from 4.8% to 3.5% with this PR. I didn't have time to do extended battery drain tests yet.

I like the "mute on focus loss" ability, since it's pretty common to see in games. This makes this PR address https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3693 for 3.x.

Some feedback:

- Since we have both `set_enabled()` and `is_enabled()` in AudioServer, we can expose an `enabled` property that would use those setter/getter methods.
author: Calinou
